speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, probably what is so amazing-- that we're the only family with six brothers and one sister survived. And we're still alive 50 years later, which is amazing and wonderful. And, and we are a very blessed family. And whilst I brought out the tragic and horrible death that I suffered-- just before we lost our son, Bill lost his wife, my brother, Bill. And she was wonderful. She had three children. And I'm very, very close to them. In fact, one of them just visited us from Vail, Colorado. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And really, it took us 25 years to get our brother out of Russia. Fortunately, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he rejoined us. And then his wife that was part of Dr. Mengele's experiments died of cancer-- horrible death. And he went for a visit to Canada. And he met a girl that he was dating before he met his wife. And he married her. And they're very happy. And really, I am looking at myself how fortunate I am in spite of the tragedies of losing my only son and losing my beloved wife, and now losing our uncles from old age. We were blessed with them for many years. One was 94. One was 89, 90. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But when I look around us, how many people really are there? Especially survivors, most of them lost their entire family, just like I lost my aunt and uncle and all the kids. And the only one that survived of 13 kids, he died.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have so many, over and over again, of survivors, that are dying, unfortunately, never even realizing that we-- that we're exposed to all kinds of diseases. And now it's catching up to them. I look at myself as an extremely lucky person to have three wonderful daughters, wonderful granddaughter, two wonderful son-in-laws. And one of them, as I said, comes from survivors. And the other one comes from an old, established family in Nashville, Tennessee. And he is absolutely wonderful to me. He is-- he's a blessing, true blessing, to my daughter and granddaughter. And it's just such a delight. And I feel that it has added so much to my life, make it more worthwhile, and makes me want to do more for America and the great country that this is in. Because the people truly here are beyond a shadow of doubt the best people in the world. I love Israel. And I'm involved with charities for Israel. But this is my country. And, and same way as I wanted to go in the Korean War to defend the United States, I would have no hesitancy if America would be in danger, to give my life for it. Because it's the bastion of freedom. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's the only place where every person, no matter what nationality, what race, what creed, what sex has an opportunity to succeed. And we should succeed. And, and the opportunities are there. But we have to see it. And I hope the kids will start to realizing-- when I say kids, I'm talking about your generation, too-- and make something out of themselves and appreciate this country, and appreciate what Israel does for the Jews, that it's very important to have Israel for ourselves. Because it's the only country will provide the one foundation, that if, God forbid, there will be a tragedy somewhere that they can go to. When anti-Semitism broke out in Russia, they were able to come to Israel. From the Arab countries, and many, many other areas, that's a home. That's a bastion of freedom for the Jewish people. And we all have to work very hard to make sure that Israel will stay a safe and a free country and-- as well as America. But we cannot be apathetic. We got to really, really spend our time, energy, and put our heart and soul into it to make sure that this country will be the bastion of freedom, not only for America, but indeed, for the entire world.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, except the remarks that I have made with Elie Wiesel, who is a dear friend of mine. That-- he's been after me to write my life story. And I gave him some excerpts. And he is so upset with me because I just have not had the time nor the energy, you know, being able to devote myself to writing my story. But he feels it's very important because I'm the first one that is able to bring out to the students and to people, whether through Kiwanis, Chamber, about the horrors that humans bestow on other humans, that we got to be guarded. And, and I will always cherish my friendship with him because he and I were brought into the camps together. And we were liberated together. And we have stayed the dearest of friends. And I just hope and pray that he will stay healthy. And we're going to continue to have the great country that we have.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, my number that was given to me Birkenau is A9018. And we had, had no name. We had no identity except this number. Nothing else mattered. We were not part of the human race anymore. The only way they checked up off every morning and every evening and many times during the week, during the day, was by our numbers. This picture shows my father's mother, grandma from Lavik. And it shows our brothers and sister when Rosalyn was just a little baby. And that's me in the little blond hair. I was probably about four years old, five years old. Next to me is-- to my left is Sam. Next to him is Bernie. And to my right is Bill. And standing up, the shorter one, is Ben, who is here in Atlanta. And the tall one is Philip, or Fishi, next to father. And of course, mother is holding little baby Rosalyn. This picture, which we found in our uncle's album, is of my beloved mother and father that were murdered in Auschwitz. This picture is also a picture found with one of my uncles. And it shows the six brothers and one sister and mother and father. And even though our parents perished in Auschwitz, the six brothers and one sister survived. And we're still alive. This picture shows the background where the boys used to sleep. And then you can see, in the back, father standing. And I'm at the bottom. I was about 14 years old at the time. And there's my brother Bill on the left. And one of the guys who used to work for us who perished in the Holocaust, and then brother Bernie, and another boy that worked for us, he also perished in the Holocaust. And my brother Sam-- and then, of course, in the background, you can see father. But the only part of the home that's left when we went back to Russia is that little home back there. The rest were all destroyed. This picture shows my Uncle Yosef to the left. He also perished in Auschwitz. And on the bottom, below him, is the shechita, or the guy who used to do the slaughtering. He also perished. And only one of his children is alive. And on top of there is my father, also perished, and my grandfather, with his white beard, and our neighbor standing. And unfortunately, not one of them is alive. This picture shows our Aunt [NON-ENGLISH], our beloved aunt, and Uncle Weiss, and their three children. And unfortunately, they all perished in Auschwitz. And we couldn't even find traces of them when we were there last year looking for them. But that was a sister of my beloved mother. And unfortunately, there's not even a trace of them. This picture shows my brother Bill. We went to visit them with the sister Rosalyn and myself to the sanatorium. He had tuberculosis. And unfortunately, I wound up in the hospital right after that, too, because we were all inflicted with all kinds of diseases. This picture shows us arriving to the orphanage homes in England. I'm with the top hat, sort of in the background. And it was one of the most wonderful feeling that we were on free grounds in England and hopefully, that we can regain our strength and health. This picture is in an orphanage home in Scotland. And I'm still very close to a lot of them. To my left is Sonyi Hoch, who has become a very successful businessmen in New York. And just above me, next to the-- between the two ladies, is [? Gene ?] [? Deutsch. ?] And I just spent with him a couple days in Cincinnati. And he worked for us for awhile. And most of those guys that you see in the pictures are still alive and healthy. And we're doing fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: --I say-- did I say that it was me with the mandolin?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead and just say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sitting there on the bottom with the mandolin because it was my favorite instrument. And all these buddies used to come in and sing with us. And it was just a wonderful, wonderful recovery. This picture shows my sister, Rosalyn. And I am to the left. And my brother Sam is to my right, just before he left for Israel to fight for our own country.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture shows me in the US Army, where I spent two wonderful years during the Korean War. And it was probably the two happiest years prior to me getting married and having a wonderful wife and children. This picture was taken when our brother, Philip, arrived from Russia. It shows how happy we were just to have him back with us and to be in a free country. The first one on the left is Sam. And then there's Rosalyn. And then there's me. And starting off from the other side is brother Ben, and then Fishi, that came from Russia, and then Bill and Bernie. This picture shows us as a family. And probably it was the happiest time of our lives. And it shows-- and I'm holding my middle daughter, Stephanie. And she was always a delight. And of course, right behind her is our son Benji, who was a very great kid, good brother. And he was very protective of his sisters. And then in the back is Etta, our oldest daughter. And she, too, made us very, very happy. And of course, then there's my beloved wife, Linda, may she rest in peace. And next to her, she's holding our youngest daughter, Robin. This picture was taken when I was honored by the Israel Bond office as the Outstanding Man in-- of the survivors. And you can see how happy we were. And she was just radiating with not only beauty, but love and what she really was.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, This picture highlights probably the greatest achievement of my life. I was given a honorary doctorate at Emory University. And of course, I'm very proud that my three lovely daughters with me, enjoying this most phenomenal occasion. Because I certainly never expected in my live to receive that kind of a honor. I'm indeed privileged and honored to introduce you to my son-in-law, [? Avi ?] Weiss, whose parents were survivors. And unfortunately, he lost his father many years ago. And his mother is still alive. And his father lost his family during the Holocaust. And so did his mother. And to my left is my daughter, Stephanie, who I'm very delighted is here in Atlanta. And we get to see each other and enjoy each other quite often. And now that I'm able to enjoy both of them, it is indeed a pleasure. And we are very, very lucky that we have each other. And God has blessed us.
